name: AI Evals for Product Managers - Voice AI Applications
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in AI Evals for Product Managers - Voice AI Applications as a competency should be able to describe the core components of a voice AI system (ASR, NLU, dialog/orchestration, NLG, TTS), explain why evaluation is essential, and distinguish between model and product/experience evaluation. They should translate product goals into evaluation objectives, define simple success criteria and metrics (task completion, error rates, latency, CSAT), and identify key use cases, user segments, and edge cases for evaluation. They must understand evaluation methodologies (offline dataset tests, manual/lab tests, basic A/B concepts), create and run simple test plans and scripts, and apply basic sampling and data logging practices while considering privacy and consent. They should use core metrics (WER, intent accuracy, containment, user effort, latency, barge-in, user ratings), segment results by user/device, and interpret pre-built dashboards or reports. They must assess prompt and dialog quality, spot common UX and model errors, and classify issues by type. They should participate in regression and smoke testing, monitor for regressions, and flag critical issues. They need to recognize safety, bias, and compliance issues, ensure test data and scenarios meet privacy and regulatory standards, and escalate risks appropriately. They should use basic tooling (test consoles, analytics dashboards, call review tools, spreadsheets, labeling/tagging tools, issue trackers), document evaluation plans and findings, and communicate results clearly to stakeholders. They must collaborate with technical, design, and analytics teams, align on evaluation scope and objectives, and incorporate feedback. They should track evaluation artifacts and learnings for reuse, ensure auditability, and follow organizational documentation and compliance practices.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Understanding of Voice AI and the Role of Evals","scope_included":"- Understands the core components of a voice AI system (ASR, NLU, dialog/orchestration, NLG, TTS) and how they work together end‑to‑end
- Recognizes key differences between voice and text/chat experiences (hands‑free use, latency sensitivity, barge‑in, wake words, background noise, interruptions)
- Understands why evaluation is essential for voice AI (quality, safety, user trust, regulatory needs, business impact)
- Can articulate the specific role of a Product Manager in AI evaluation: defining goals, success criteria, target segments, and prioritizing issues rather than designing models
- Differentiates between model performance evaluation and product/experience evaluation, and understands how they inform each other
- Understands the lifecycle of a voice AI feature (discovery, design, offline eval, pilot, online eval, monitoring, iteration) and where different eval activities fit
- Recognizes typical constraints and trade‑offs in voice AI (accuracy vs latency vs coverage vs cost) that evaluations must help quantify","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Evaluation Objectives and Success Criteria for Voice AI Products","scope_included":"- Translates product goals and user value propositions into clear evaluation objectives (e.g., improved task completion, reduced friction, higher containment)
- Distinguishes between business KPIs, user experience outcomes, and technical metrics, and understands how each is used in evaluation
- Defines concrete success criteria for a feature or release (e.g., target ranges for task success, error rates, latency, CSAT) with guidance from more senior staff
- Ensures evaluation goals are aligned with product strategy, OKRs, and user needs, not just what is easy to measure
- Understands leading vs lagging indicators (e.g., task success as leading indicator for churn) and how to select relevant indicators
- Identifies primary vs secondary metrics for a given evaluation (e.g., prioritize understanding for a support bot vs speed for a voice assistant)
- Recognizes when metrics conflict (e.g., stricter safety vs higher completion) and surfaces trade‑offs for decision‑makers
- Ensures success criteria are realistic given current model capabilities, data constraints, and timeline","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"User-Centered Scenarios, Use Cases, and Data Scoping","scope_included":"- Derives evaluation scenarios from user personas, jobs‑to‑be‑done, and real user journeys specific to voice interactions
- Identifies core use cases, frequent user intents, and high‑value flows that must be covered in evaluation
- Identifies critical edge cases and failure‑prone scenarios for voice AI (noisy environments, strong accents, speech impairments, multi‑speaker situations, overlapping speech)
- Ensures evaluation coverage across user segments (age, accent, language variety, technical proficiency) in line with product’s target audience and fairness goals
- Specifies high‑level data requirements for evaluation (volume, diversity, languages, devices, environments) and validates that they map to real usage
- Understands the difference between training data, evaluation data, and live traffic; ensures evaluation data is kept separate from training where required
- Defines acceptance criteria for user journeys (what counts as successful completion vs partial vs failure) in measurable terms
- Identifies which user actions and system events need to be logged for later evaluation (utterances, transcripts, intents, latency, interruptions, transfers to human)
- Considers privacy constraints and user consent implications when scoping evaluation data (what can and cannot be logged/retained)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Evaluation Methodologies and Test Design for Voice AI","scope_included":"- Understands the main categories of evaluation for voice AI: offline evaluations (using static datasets), lab/usability tests, and online experiments
- Designs simple evaluation plans that specify scope, objectives, metrics, user segments, and timelines for a feature
- Creates basic test sets and scenario libraries that reflect real user utterances, including paraphrases and variations in phrasing
- Differentiates between golden‑set evaluations (curated labeled data) and ad‑hoc/manual testing, and uses both appropriately
- Understands the concept of train/validation/test splits at a conceptual level and why data leakage undermines evaluation credibility
- Plans and runs structured manual test sessions of voice experiences, following a defined script and capturing consistent observations
- Applies simple sampling strategies (e.g., random sampling, stratified by intent or user segment) when reviewing logs or calls for evaluation
- Understands high‑level A/B testing concepts for voice features (control vs treatment, consistent randomization, exposure) without deep statistical analysis
- Recognizes limitations of small‑sample manual tests and lab environments, and knows when larger‑scale or online evaluations are needed
- Ensures evaluations are repeatable by standardizing test conditions as much as practical (devices, environments, prompts/scripts)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Metrics and Analytics for Voice AI Evaluation","scope_included":"- Understands and uses core performance metrics for ASR quality at a conceptual level (e.g., word error rate, sentence error rate) and what typical improvements mean in practice
- Understands basic NLU and intent detection metrics conceptually (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and how misclassification impacts user experience
- Works with task/flow‑level metrics: task completion rate, containment rate (self‑service vs human agent), first contact resolution (where relevant)
- Interprets experience metrics: user effort (number of turns, repetition rate), escalation/transfer rate, drop‑off/abandonment in multi‑turn flows
- Uses conversational UX metrics relevant to voice: latency (first response and turn‑to‑turn), barge‑in success, wake word detection success, false accept and false reject rates
- Incorporates user‑reported metrics (CSAT, NPS, post‑call surveys, thumbs up/down) into evaluation and understands their limitations and biases
- Calculates simple metrics and aggregates from raw counts (rates, percentages, averages) using spreadsheets or basic dashboard tools
- Segments metrics by key dimensions (intent, device type, language, environment, time of day, user segment) to detect patterns of underperformance
- Differentiates between statistical noise and clear performance shifts at a basic level (large changes vs small fluctuations) and escalates borderline cases
- Reads and interprets pre‑built dashboards and reports, and asks targeted questions to analytics/data science partners for deeper investigation","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Conversation, Prompting, and Dialog Flow Assessment","scope_included":"- Evaluates conversation quality across the full interaction: clarity of system prompts, naturalness, coherence across turns, and satisfaction at resolution
- Assesses whether voice prompts are concise, speakable, and optimized for listening rather than reading (short, front‑loaded, minimal cognitive load)
- Checks that prompts set correct expectations, guide users effectively, and include clear options and confirmations when needed
- Evaluates how well the system handles misunderstandings and errors: reprompts, confirmations, fallback behaviors, and escalation paths
- Identifies common UX issues in voice conversations (overly long prompts, frequent interruptions, repetitive confirmations, dead ends) and flags them systematically
- Understands basic prompt design concepts for LLM‑based or NLU‑based voice systems (e.g., instructions, context framing, constraints) and can propose simple prompt refinements
- Evaluates multi‑turn dialogs for context retention (reference resolution, follow‑up questions, pronouns, ellipsis) at a user‑impact level
- Reviews dialog flows for consistency across entry points and channels (e.g., voice vs chat) and flags inconsistencies that impact user trust
- Classifies observed issues into categories (recognition errors, intent mapping errors, poor prompt design, flow logic issues, policy/safety issues) to aid triage
- Works with designers and conversation designers to align evaluation criteria with conversation style guides and tone‑of‑voice standards","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Model and System Behavior Understanding for Evaluation","scope_included":"- Understands, at a conceptual level, how ASR, NLU, dialog policies, and TTS each contribute to user‑perceived quality and where each can fail
- Recognizes typical ASR failure modes: substitutions, deletions, insertions, difficulty with names/entities, accent and noise sensitivity, and overlapping speech
- Recognizes typical NLU and dialog issues: wrong intent, missing entities/slots, inappropriate fallbacks, inconsistent responses, poor disambiguation
- Understands TTS quality dimensions: intelligibility, prosody, speaking rate, emphasis, emotional tone consistency, and how these affect user trust
- Identifies when problems can be addressed via prompt changes, flow changes, config changes, or data/labeling adjustments versus needing deeper model retraining
- Appreciates limitations of current models (e.g., difficulty with code‑switching, domain‑specific jargon, long context retention) and reflects them in evaluation expectations
- Distinguishes between deterministic rule‑based behavior and probabilistic model behavior, and understands why evaluations must consider variability
- Understands the impact of device and channel constraints (telephony bandwidth, smart speakers, mobile apps, car systems) on perceived model performance
- Recognizes integration issues that can mimic model problems (API timeouts, misconfigured endpoints, logging gaps) and ensures they are considered during evaluation
- Knows when to escalate anomalous behaviors for deeper technical investigation rather than over‑interpreting small datasets or anecdotal evidence","is_non_core_section...
